(**MSA**) is a [geographical region](/wiki/Geographical_region "Geographical region") with a relatively high [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") at its [core](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") and close economic ties throughout the region.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Such regions are not legally [incorporated as a city or town would be](/wiki/Incorporated_town "Incorporated town") and are not legal [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") like [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") or separate entities such as [states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"). As a result, sometimes the precise definition of a given [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") will vary between sources. The statistical criteria for a standard metropolitan area were defined in 1949 and redefined as a metropolitan statistical area in 1983.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), a **metropolitan statistical area** (**MSA**) is a [geographical region](/wiki/Geographical_region "Geographical region") with a relatively high [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") at its [core](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area") and close economic ties throughout the region.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Such regions are not legally [incorporated as a city or town would be](/wiki/Incorporated_town "Incorporated town") and are not legal [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") like [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") or separate entities such as [states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"). As a result, sometimes the precise definition of a given [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") will vary between sources. The statistical criteria for a standard metropolitan area were defined in 1949 and redefined as a metropolitan statistical area in 1983.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Due to [suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization"), the typical metropolitan area is polycentric rather than being centered around a large historic core city such as [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") or [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Some metropolitan areas include more than one large historic core city; examples include the [Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"), [Virginia Beach–Norfolk–Newport News (Hampton Roads)](/wiki/Hampton_Roads "Hampton Roads"), [Riverside–San Bernardino (Inland Empire)](/wiki/Inland_Empire "Inland Empire"), and [Minneapolis–Saint Paul (Twin Cities)](/wiki/Minneapolis%E2%80%93Saint_Paul "Minneapolis–Saint Paul").

MSAs are defined by the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget") (OMB), which is part of the [Executive Office of the President](/wiki/Executive_Office_of_the_President_of_the_United_States "Executive Office of the President of the United States"), and are used by the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") and other [U.S. federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") agencies for statistical purposes.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_statistical_area&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definitions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/CBSA_WallMap_Jul2023.pdf/page1-250px-CBSA_WallMap_Jul2023.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:CBSA_WallMap_Jul2023.pdf)

An enlargeable map of the 935 [core-based statistical areas](/wiki/Core-based_statistical_area "Core-based statistical area") (CBSAs) of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") as of 2023; the 393 MSAs are shown in green.[[6]](#cite_note-Revision_2023-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Metro_area_populations.svg/250px-Metro_area_populations.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Metro_area_populations.svg)

Metro area populations

The U.S. [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget") defines a set of [core-based statistical areas](/wiki/Core-based_statistical_area "Core-based statistical area") (CBSAs) throughout the country, which are composed of [counties and county equivalents](/wiki/List_of_United_States_counties_and_county_equivalents "List of United States counties and county equivalents").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

CBSAs are delineated on the basis of a central contiguous area of relatively high population density, known as an [urban area](/wiki/United_States_urban_area "United States urban area"). The counties containing the core urban area are known as the "central counties" of the CBSA; these are defined as having at least 50% of their population living in [urban areas](/wiki/List_of_United_States_urban_areas "List of United States urban areas") of at least 10,000 in population.[[8]](#cite_note-OFR-8) Additional surrounding counties, known as "outlying counties", can be included in the CBSA if these counties have strong social and economic ties to the central county or counties as measured by [commuting](/wiki/Commuting "Commuting") and employment. Outlying counties are included in the CBSA if 25% of the workers living in the county work in the central county or counties, or if 25% of the employment in the county is held by workers who live in the central county or counties.

Adjacent CBSAs are merged into a single CBSA when the central county or counties of one CBSA qualify as an outlying county or counties to the other CBSAs.[[8]](#cite_note-OFR-8) One or more CBSAs may be grouped together or combined to form a larger statistical entity known as a [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") (CSA) when the employment interchange measure (EIM) reaches 15% or more.

CBSAs are subdivided into MSAs (formed around urban areas of at least 50,000 in population) and [micropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Micropolitan_statistical_area "Micropolitan statistical area") (μSAs), which are CBSAs built around an urban area of at least 10,000 in population but less than 50,000 in population. Some metropolitan areas may include multiple cities below 50,000 people, but combined have over 50,000 people.[[8]](#cite_note-OFR-8) Previous terms that are no longer used to describe these regions include "standard metropolitan statistical area" (SMSA) and "primary metropolitan statistical area" (PMSA).[[9]](#cite_note-9) Metropolitan statistical areas containing over 2.5 million people can also be further divided into metropolitan divisions.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On January 19, 2021, OMB submitted a regulation for public comment that would increase the minimum population needed for an urban area population to be a metropolitan statistical area to be increased from 50,000 to 100,000.[[11]](#cite_note-11) It ultimately decided to keep the minimum at 50,000 for the 2020 cycle.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

On July 21, 2023, the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget") released revised delineations of the various CBSAs in the United States.[[6]](#cite_note-Revision_2023-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_statistical_area&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The Census Bureau created the metropolitan district for the [1910 census](/wiki/1910_United_States_census "1910 United States census") as a standardized classification for large urban centers and their surrounding areas. The original threshold for a metropolitan district was 200,000, but was lowered to 100,000 in 1930 and 50,000 in 1940.[[13]](#cite_note-CES-Definition-13) The metropolitan districts were replaced by standard metropolitan areas (SMAs) in the [1950 census](/wiki/1950_United_States_census "1950 United States census"), which were defined by the Bureau of the Budget (now the Office of Management and Budget) and later renamed to standard metropolitan statistical areas (SMAs) in 1959.[[13]](#cite_note-CES-Definition-13)[[14]](#cite_note-History-14) The modern metropolitan statistical area was created in 1983 amid a large increase in the number of eligible markets, which grew from 172 in 1950 to 288 in 1980;[[13]](#cite_note-CES-Definition-13)[[15]](#cite_note-15) the core based statistical area (CBSA) was introduced in 2000 and defined in 2003 with a minimum population of 10,000 required for micropolitan areas and 50,000 for urban areas.[[13]](#cite_note-CES-Definition-13)[[14]](#cite_note-History-14)

## Puerto Rico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_statistical_area&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Puerto Rico")]

This [sortable](/wiki/Help:Sortable_tables "Help:Sortable tables") [table](/wiki/Help:Table "Help:Table") lists the six [metropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_areas "Metropolitan statistical areas") (MSAs) of [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") including:

1. The MSA rank by population as of July 1, 2025, as estimated by the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau")[[16]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-16)
2. The MSA name as designated by the [United States Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_Management_and_Budget "United States Office of Management and Budget")[[6]](#cite_note-Revision_2023-6)
3. The MSA population as of July 1, 2025, as estimated by the United States Census Bureau[[16]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-16)
4. The MSA population as of April 1, 2020, as enumerated by the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census")[[16]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-16)[[a]](#cite_note-2023Adj-17)
5. The percent MSA population change from April 1, 2020, to July 1, 2025[[16]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-16)
6. The [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") (CSA)[[b]](#cite_note-CSA-18) if the MSA is a component[[17]](#cite_note-OMB_20-01-19)
